title: Optimize Batch Status DB Query
description: Replace in-memory aggregation of batch status with a SQL GROUP BY query to prevent memory spikes and high Garbage Collection (GC) pressure when dealing with large document batches.
steps: Implement a GORM query using `.Select("status, count(*) as count").Where("batch_id = ?", batchID).Group("status")` to retrieve grouped statistics.
Update the batch progress calculation to use the aggregated counts instead of instantiating and iterating over thousands of job models in memory.
Retain the fast path for retrieving specific paginated file statuses.

title: Eliminate Base64 Image Payloads in Workflow
description: Images are currently base64 encoded and passed through Temporal workflows and internal HTTP/gRPC requests. This inflates data size by 33% and causes massive CPU/memory bottlenecks. Pass MinIO object paths directly to the ML services.
steps: Update the API Gateway and Temporal Worker (`app/activities.go`) to pass the `storage_path` (MinIO URI) to the ML services instead of reading files into memory and base64 encoding them.
Modify `glm-ocr-service/app/main.py` and `glm_inference.py` to accept `image_path` or `image_url` instead of `image` (base64 string).
Update the Python backend to resolve the MinIO URL or attached shared volume to read the raw bytes directly using `io.BytesIO` or `PIL.Image.open`.
Remove Base64 encoding/decoding overhead across the pipeline.

title: Remove Triton stringToUint8Tensor Workaround
description: The `stringToUint8Tensor` function in the Temporal worker converts JSON strings into an array of integers as a workaround for a Triton bug. This causes enormous JSON payload bloat and serialization delays.
steps: Validate Triton Server and Python Backend version. Upgrade to version >= 24.x if necessary where BYTES handling is fixed.
Refactor `callTritonHTTP` in `temporal-worker/app/activities.go` to pass strings natively instead of casting characters to integers.
Ensure the corresponding Triton Python model handles the native BYTES tensor appropriately.

title: Refactor GPU Lock to Capacity Semaphore
description: The current `AcquireGPULock` acts as a strict global mutex (likely via Redis). This severely limits throughput by forcing GPUs to process jobs sequentially. Replace this with a token-bucket or capacity semaphore.
steps: Transform the mutex lock into a Redis-backed capacity semaphore that allows N concurrent jobs, bounded by total GPU VRAM and `max_parallel_regions`.
Allow Triton Inference Server's built-in dynamic batching to handle localized request queuing without forcing the Temporal orchestrator into an artificial wait loop.
